hook: The Almohads' monumental kasbah gate, carved with interlacing geometry and a Kufic war-verse — and a rare, almost-hidden frieze of serpents.
facts: Built between 1195 and 1199 by the Almohad caliph Abu Yusuf Ya'qub al-Mansur, added to the kasbah walls his predecessor Abd al-Mu'min raised around 1150.
Its outer facade carries a Kufic inscription frieze quoting Surat As-Saff (61:9-13), and a curved band of interlacing geometric forms around the horseshoe arch.
The decoration includes serpentine 'S'-shaped forms read as eels, described as a very rare motif in Almohad and Moroccan architecture.
By the 18th century, under the Alaouite dynasty, the gate had been repurposed as a prison.
The Kasbah des Oudayas, of which the gate is the main entrance, was inscribed as part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2012.
w: The Almohads' monumental kasbah gate of the 1190s, its horseshoe arch wrapped in interlacing geometry and a Kufic war-verse from the Quran -- and, almost hidden among the carving, a band of serpents, a motif vanishingly rare in Moroccan stone. By the 18th century it had become a prison. The grand front door of the blue-and-white citadel.

hook: A tiled terrace café on the kasbah's seaward rampart where generations have drunk mint tea over the Bou Regreg, watching Salé across the water.
facts: The café sits on the rampart of the Kasbah des Oudayas above the Bou Regreg estuary, beside the Andalusian Gardens, looking across the river to Salé.
Its terrace is laid out with zellij (mosaic tilework) benches and serves mint tea with ghriba almond cakes.
It adjoins the Andalusian Gardens laid out in 1912 on General Lyautey's orders on the site of the kasbah's former mechouar (royal courtyard).
It has also operated under the name Café des Oudayas.
w: A tiled terrace on the kasbah's seaward rampart where Rabat has drunk mint tea over the Bou Regreg for generations, watching Sale shimmer across the water. Zellij benches, ghriba almond cakes, and the estuary breeze. It sits beside the Andalusian Gardens, above the river mouth. The slowest, sweetest view in the city.

hook: A walled Moorish garden of roses and fruit trees, planted by the French inside a 12th-century citadel and modelled on the Alhambra and Seville.
facts: Laid out in 1912 on the orders of General Lyautey, on the site of the kasbah's former mechouar (royal courtyard), to plans by architect Maurice Tranchant de Lunel.
In 1919 the landscape gardener Jean-Claude-Nicolas Forestier gave it its present form, drawing on the Andalusian gardens of Seville and the Alhambra.
It is enclosed within the ancient walls of the Kasbah des Oudayas, a UNESCO World Heritage site.
The garden adjoins the Oudayas Museum, housed in a 17th-century palace, and the Café Maure terrace over the estuary.
w: A walled Moorish garden of roses, oranges and shade inside a 12th-century citadel -- planted by the French in 1912 on the kasbah's old royal courtyard and shaped into its present form, after the Alhambra and Seville, by the landscaper Forestier. Cats doze among the fountains. A piece of lost Andalusia, replanted behind Almohad walls.

hook: The oldest mosque in Rabat — an Almohad foundation of 1150 later rebuilt by an English corsair turned Muslim convert.
facts: Known as Jami' al-Atiq, it was built around 1150 (544 AH) when the Almohads rebuilt the Oudayas kasbah, and is the oldest mosque in Rabat.
A marble plaque on the facade attributes its construction to Abd al-Mu'min ibn Ali in 544 AH.
Its prayer hall has a T-shaped plan of seven naves and three bays, with a wider axial nave along the qibla wall, typical of Almohad architecture.
It was largely rebuilt in the 18th century by an English corsair and convert known as Ahmed el-Inglizi ('the Englishman').
It stands on Rue Jamaa, roughly 200 m inside the kasbah from Bab Oudaia.
w: The oldest mosque in Rabat, an Almohad foundation of 1150 raised when the caliph rebuilt the Oudayas kasbah -- and, oddly, rebuilt again in the 1700s by an English corsair who had turned Muslim, Ahmed el-Inglizi, 'the Englishman'. Its hall keeps the classic T-shaped Almohad plan. A pirate's renovation, two hundred metres inside the gate.

hook: A pleasure-palace built for Sultan Moulay Ismail inside the kasbah, now Morocco's national jewellery museum after a century as an ethnographic collection.
facts: The building is a former princely residence built around 1672-1694 on the orders of the Alaouite Sultan Moulay Ismail, in Andalusian style with a courtyard garden.
An ethnographic Oudaya Museum of carpets, ceramics and instruments opened in the palace in 1915.
In 2002 it was re-conceived under the National Foundation of Museums as the National Jewellery Museum.
It reopened in early 2023 after a roughly two-year restoration, with a collection of some 8,000 objects spanning prehistory to Islamic gold and silver work.
w: A pleasure-palace built inside the kasbah for Sultan Moulay Ismail in the 1670s, around a courtyard garden -- an ethnographic museum for a century, and since 2023 Morocco's national jewellery museum. Some 8,000 objects run from prehistoric beads to Islamic gold and silver. A sultan's retreat, refilled with the country's finest metalwork.

hook: The medina's arcaded carpet street, where foreign consuls were once required to live so they could ransom captives from the Bou Regreg corsairs.
facts: The street runs roughly a kilometre through the medina and was laid out so that foreign diplomats had to reside there.
The placement put the consuls beside the captives' negotiation zone: prisoners taken by the Salé corsairs were resold nearby at Souk El Ghazal, and by treaty diplomats had the right to buy them back.
The Medina of Rabat itself was largely built up from the 17th century by Moriscos (Andalusian Muslims) expelled from Spain by Philip III.
Today the street is the heart of Rabat's Rbati carpet workshops and silver ateliers, lined with arcades of rugs, pottery, jewellery and leather.
w: The medina's arcaded carpet street -- and once the only street foreign consuls could live on, beside the market where the Bou Regreg corsairs resold their captives, so the diplomats could ransom them. Moriscos from Spain built much of the quarter. It runs on rugs, silver and leather now. Diplomacy, on the slave trade's doorstep.

hook: A red-courtyard synagogue in Rabat's old Jewish quarter, built into the house of a revered 19th-century rabbi against the medina's Andalusian wall.
facts: The synagogue stands in the Mellah (Jewish quarter) of Rabat's medina, near Bab Diouana and contiguous to the Andalusian wall of the medina.
It occupies the former residence of Rabbi Shalom Zaoui (c. 1839-1918), revered by his community, and is named after him.
Its Moorish design includes three-lobed window shapes and mosque-style lamps; visitors enter across a courtyard painted bright red.
It is listed as an active synagogue.
w: A synagogue built into the house of a revered 19th-century rabbi, tucked against the medina's old Andalusian wall in Rabat's Mellah -- its courtyard a startling bright red, its windows three-lobed, mosque-style lamps hung within. Named for Shalom Zaoui, who lived and taught here. A red door into the old Jewish quarter.

hook: Acres of leaning white headstones spilling from the kasbah's red walls down to the Atlantic, where Edith Wharton found 'infinite sadness' under a fading sky.
facts: The cemetery lies south of the Kasbah des Oudayas on a cliff that descends toward a beach at the mouth of the Bou Regreg.

Edith Wharton, in 'In Morocco' (1920), wrote that 'acres and acres of graves fall away from the red ramparts to the grey sea; and breakers rolling straight from America send their spray across the lowest stones.'
She described 'infinite sadness in this scene under the fading sky, beside the cold welter of the Atlantic.'
It is one of the largest Muslim cemeteries in Rabat, occupying the seafront slope between the kasbah and the ocean.
w: Acres of leaning white headstones spilling from the kasbah's red ramparts down to the Atlantic. Edith Wharton, here in 1920, wrote of 'acres and acres of graves' falling to the grey sea, breakers rolling straight from America across the lowest stones. She found 'infinite sadness' in it. One of Rabat's largest cemeteries, facing the ocean.

hook: The stump of a 12th-century minaret that was meant to crown the largest mosque in the western Islamic world, abandoned mid-build and ringed by a field of broken columns.
facts: Commissioned by the Almohad caliph Abu Yusuf Yaqub al-Mansur; the minaret and mosque were built roughly 1191-1199 CE.
The tower stands about 44 m tall but was intended to reach at least 64 m (some estimates ~80 m), which would have made it among the tallest minarets of its time.
Work stopped when al-Mansur died in 1199, leaving the mosque unfinished; only the beginnings of walls and 348 stone columns were ever raised.
Like the Giralda in Seville and the Koutoubia in Marrakesh, it was built with internal ramps instead of stairs, wide enough for a rider to ascend on horseback.
The minaret is built of reddish sandstone and forms part of the UNESCO World Heritage 'Rabat, Modern Capital and Historic City' inscription (2012).
w: The stump of a minaret meant to crown the largest mosque in the western Islamic world, abandoned in 1199 when its Almohad patron died -- leaving a 44-metre tower and a field of 348 broken columns where the prayer hall never rose. Like the Giralda and the Koutoubia, it climbs by internal ramp, wide enough to ride a horse up.

a: Yacoub al-Mansour Esplanade (opposite the Hassan Tower)
hook: A white onyx-and-marble royal tomb facing the Hassan Tower across the column field, its gates flanked by mounted royal guards in scarlet.
facts: Built 1961-1971 to house the tomb of Sultan/King Mohammed V (died 1961), who led Morocco to independence from France in 1956.
Designed by the Vietnamese architect Eric (Cong) Vo Toan in a traditional Moroccan idiom, with a carved Atlas-cedar ceiling and a white onyx cenotaph.
It now also holds the tombs of King Hassan II (died 1999) and Prince Moulay Abdallah.
Both entrances to the esplanade are flanked by mounted members of the Moroccan Royal Guard in red uniforms; the building is one of the few mosques/mausoleums in Morocco non-Muslims may enter.
It stands opposite the Hassan Tower and shares the UNESCO World Heritage listing of historic Rabat.
w: A white onyx-and-marble royal tomb facing the Hassan Tower across the column field, under a carved cedar ceiling -- the resting place of the king who led Morocco to independence, raised 1961-71 by a Vietnamese architect. Mounted royal guards in scarlet flank the gates. One of the few Moroccan mausoleums non-Muslims may enter.

a: Bab Zaer, south of the city walls
hook: A walled Marinid necropolis built over a buried Roman town, where storks nest on a ruined minaret above a pool of supposedly sacred eels.
facts: The site holds the ruins of the Roman port-town of Sala Colonia, with a decumanus maximus, forum and triumphal arch; it gained municipal status around the mid-2nd century AD.
In the 14th century the Marinid sultan Abu al-Hasan enclosed the site as a royal necropolis, adding a mosque, madrasa and mausoleums; the main gate (Bab Zaer) was completed in 1339.
A ruined minaret about 15 m tall survives, decorated with sebka patterning, and is a favoured nesting site for the colony of white storks that breed across the ruins.
Abu al-Hasan's own richly carved tomb is the best-preserved of the Marinid mausoleums on the site.
Chellah is part of the UNESCO World Heritage inscription of Rabat (2012).
legend: A spring-fed pool, the Bassin aux Anguilles, is said to be home to sacred eels; local tradition holds that women who feed boiled eggs to the eels will be granted fertility and an easy childbirth.
w: A walled Marinid necropolis built over a buried Roman town -- the forum of Sala Colonia below, royal tombs above, storks nesting on every height. At its heart a spring-fed pool of eels: feed them a boiled egg, the tradition runs, and you'll be granted children and an easy birth. Ruins, storks and a fertility pool in one wall.

hook: The 'Gate of the Winds', a monumental Almohad portal whose red stone the Atlantic gales have weathered to grey, now an art gallery.
facts: Built by the Almohad caliph Ya'qub al-Mansur in the late 12th century, with the gate and adjoining ramparts finished by 1197.
Its name means 'Gate of the Winds', a reference to the strong Atlantic winds; the originally reddish stone has weathered to a duller grey.
The facade is carved with three concentric semi-circles of polylobed and radiating motifs and a Kufic inscription bearing Quran 3:104-5, comparable to Bab Oudaia in Rabat and Bab Agnaou in Marrakesh.
Its interior passage uses a bent (dog-leg) entrance typical of Almohad military architecture; the passage was partly filled in during the 18th century under Sultan Muhammad ibn Abdallah.
Since 1960 the interior has served as an art gallery (Galerie Bab Rouah), renovated by the Ministry of Culture around 2001-2002.
w: The 'Gate of the Winds' -- a monumental Almohad portal of the 1190s whose red stone the Atlantic gales have scoured to grey. Its facade carves three concentric arcs of lobed and radiating ornament around a Quranic frieze; inside, the passage doglegs, a soldier's trick to break a charge. A war gate that has been an art gallery since 1960.

a: Place Al Joulane (Place du Golan), Ville Nouvelle
hook: A white Art Deco church with twin pointed towers rising over a downtown square, blending 1920s geometry with Moorish detail.
facts: Construction began in 1919 to designs by the French architect Adrien Laforgue; it was inaugurated by Resident-General Hubert Lyautey in 1921.
Its two distinctive towers were added in the 1930s, completing the silhouette seen today.
The building is a hallmark of the protectorate-era Art Deco style softened with Mauresque (Moorish) elements, including Art Deco geometric floor decoration.
It is the cathedral and ecclesiastical seat of the Catholic Archdiocese of Rabat and remains an active church.
It stands on Place Al Joulane (formerly Place du Golan) in the colonial Ville Nouvelle.
w: A white Art Deco church with twin pointed towers rising over a downtown square, its 1920s geometry softened with Moorish detail. Adrien Laforgue designed it; Lyautey opened it in 1921, and the towers came a decade later. It serves as the cathedral of Catholic Rabat to this day. Deco, with a Mauresque accent, in the colonial new town.

n: Mohammed VI Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art (MMVI)
hook: Morocco's first big new museum since independence, a white double-arched palace of modern Moroccan and African art.
facts: Opened on 7 October 2014 after roughly a decade of construction, it was the first large-scale museum built in Morocco since independence in 1956.
Designed by Moroccan architect Karim Chakor, the three-level building covers about 9,400 m2 and blends Arab-Moorish elements with a modern white double-arched facade.
Its collection features leading modern Moroccan artists such as Farid Belkahia, Mohamed Melehi and Chaibia Talal.
It hosts major touring exhibitions and houses an auditorium, libraries, a conservation lab and an education department.
It stands in the Ville Nouvelle on Avenue Moulay Hassan.
w: Morocco's first big new museum since independence -- a white, double-arched palace of modern Moroccan and African art, opened in 2014. Karim Chakor designed it, marrying Arab-Moorish forms to a clean modern face. Belkahia, Melehi and Chaibia hang inside. The country's modern eye, given a home in the capital.

hook: A 1914 colonial test garden laid out on the old royal Agdal orchards, designed to acclimatise exotic plants and to show off French botanical prestige.
facts: Created in 1914 during the French Protectorate, after Lyautey moved Morocco's capital to Rabat; landscape designs were by Jean-Claude-Nicolas Forestier.
It was built on the former Agdal of the royal palace, terraced down a slope to ease irrigation in the manner of a traditional Islamic garden.
It spans about 17 hectares and originally held over 250 varieties of fruit and ornamental trees gathered from around the world.
It served a dual purpose: a working acclimatisation/research garden and a showcase of French botanical science in service of colonial power.
After closing in 2006 it was restored and reopened to the public in June 2013 (inaugurated by King Mohammed VI); it is now managed by Morocco's INRA agronomic research institute.
w: A 1914 colonial test garden terraced down the old royal Agdal orchards -- part lab to acclimatise exotic trees, part showcase of French botanical prestige. Forestier laid it out; it once held over 250 kinds of tree. Closed, then restored and reopened in 2013. Empire's botany, gone to public shade.

a: Ville Nouvelle, between Bab el-Had and the Hassan Tower quarter
hook: The grand colonial boulevard once called Cours Lyautey, a parade of white Art Deco and Mauresque civic palaces along Henri Prost's grid.
facts: The main avenue was developed mostly between 1915 and 1932 under the French Protectorate and was originally known as Cours Lyautey.
It is the spine of the Ville Nouvelle laid out south of the medina by urban planner Henri Prost, under Resident-General Hubert Lyautey, with wide boulevards and a rational grid.
Its central post office was designed by Adrien Laforgue and Albert Laprade and completed in 1918.
The Bank Al-Maghrib headquarters on the avenue was designed by Auguste Cadet and Edmond Brion and completed in 1925.
The avenue and its Mauresque public buildings form part of the UNESCO World Heritage 'Rabat, Modern Capital and Historic City' inscription (2012).
w: The grand colonial boulevard once called Cours Lyautey, the spine of the planned new town -- a parade of white Art Deco and Mauresque civic palaces down Henri Prost's rational grid. The 1918 post office and 1925 central bank still line it. France laid out a whole capital here in two decades. A boulevard built to look like power.

hook: A monumental Marinid sea-gate so tall that war-galleys once sailed straight off the Bou Regreg, through its horseshoe arch, into a hidden inner dockyard.
facts: Built in the 1260s–1280s under Marinid sultan Abu Yusuf Yaqub ibn Abd al-Haqq, reportedly designed by Andalusian architect Muhammad ibn Ali al-Ashbili.
Its name means 'gate of the little port'; a canal linked the fortified Salé arsenal to the Bou Regreg so ships could pass directly through the gate into a protected inner dock.
The horseshoe-arch portal stands about 9.6 metres high, flanked by two oblong defensive towers and carved with floral knotwork and inscriptions.
The fortified port was built in the wake of a devastating Castilian raid on Salé in 1260.
Silting of the canal eventually left the gate stranded inland, away from the water it once served.
w: A Marinid sea-gate so tall that war-galleys once sailed straight off the Bou Regreg, through its 9-metre horseshoe arch, into a hidden inner dockyard. Sale built it in the 1270s after a Castilian raid gutted the port, a canal feeding ships right up to the wall. The river has long since silted away, leaving the gate marooned inland.

hook: A jewel-box Marinid Quranic college beside the Great Mosque, every surface dripping with zellij, carved stucco and cedarwood since the 1340s.
facts: Commissioned by Marinid sultan Abu al-Hasan; an entrance inscription dates the start to 1332–1333 and a marble foundation plaque records completion in 1341–1342.
Built to feed the adjacent Great Mosque of Salé as the city's centre of religious and intellectual life.
Decoration ascends from zellij tilework on floor and lower walls to carved stucco and then carved cedar surfaces, with calligraphic, geometric, arabesque and floral motifs.
A rectangular courtyard is ringed by galleries beneath two upper storeys of small cells that once housed students.
The prayer hall is roofed with a central dome and edged by a carved wooden frieze.
w: A jewel-box Marinid college beside Sale's Great Mosque, every surface worked since the 1340s -- zellij tile underfoot, carved stucco above it, cedarwood crowning the lot, all calligraphy and arabesque. Built by sultan Abu al-Hasan to feed the mosque next door with scholars, its tiny upper cells once housed the students.

n: Zaouia of Sidi Abdellah ben Hassoun
hook: The shrine of Salé's patron saint, where on the eve of the Prophet's birthday a torchlit crowd carries man-high lanterns of carved, gilded wax to be left for a year.
facts: Sidi Abdellah ben Hassoun (c.1515 Fes – 1604 Salé) is venerated as the patron saint of Salé and of travellers; his zaouia sits just behind the Great Mosque.
The dome over his tomb is attributed to the Alaouite sultan Moulay Ismail.

On the eve of Mawlid, the Dour Ach-Chmaa candle procession carries giant decorated wax lanterns – some over two metres tall, gilded with gold leaf – past the Great Mosque to his shrine, where they are deposited for a year until the next Mawlid.
Tradition holds the festival was introduced under Saadian sultan Ahmad al-Mansur (late 16th century), inspired by candle processions he saw during exile in Ottoman Istanbul.
legend: The lanterns left in the mausoleum are said to keep the saint's baraka (blessing) circulating through the city for the whole year until they are renewed.
w: The shrine of Sale's patron saint, where on the eve of the Prophet's birthday a torchlit crowd carries giant lanterns of carved, gilded wax -- some taller than a man -- past the Great Mosque to lay them at his tomb for a year. The Dour Ach-Chmaa candle procession is said to date from a sultan's exile in Ottoman Istanbul.

hook: A vast white necropolis tumbling toward the Atlantic around the tomb of a 14th-century healer-saint, beside whom the sick and the mad once came to sleep for a cure.
facts: Named for Sidi Ben Achir al-Andaloussi (Ahmed ben Mohammed ben Omar ben Achir), an Andalusian-born Sufi saint who settled in Salé and died in 1364.
He is counted among Salé's four major saints and was renowned for claimed healing of blindness, deafness and mental illness.
Sultan Moulay Abdellah ibn Ismail raised a dome over the tomb; Sultan Abderrahmane ibn Hicham later built a nearby maristan (hospital) of about thirty rooms for the sick, especially the mentally ill.
The surrounding cemetery covers roughly 13.5 hectares on the Atlantic shore, the ocean to one side and the Kasbah of the Oudayas across the river to the south.
legend: Pilgrims believed the saint's baraka could cure the incurable; the afflicted, including the mentally ill, would stay beside the shrine hoping his blessing would heal them.
w: A vast white necropolis tumbling toward the Atlantic around the tomb of a 14th-century healer-saint -- and once a place of cure, where the sick and the mentally ill came to sleep beside the shrine hoping his blessing would heal them. A sultan even built a small hospital here for them. Thirteen hectares of graves, the ocean alongside.

hook: The 'Bastion of Tears' on the Salé bank, a 17th-century fort guarding the river mouth in the age of the Sallee Rover corsairs and their human cargo.
facts: A 17th-century bastion overlooking the mouth of the Bou Regreg, dominating the river it once defended.
It dates from the era when Salé was a base of the Sallee Rovers and the corsair Republic of Bou Regreg (1627–1668), whose economy ran on piracy and the ransom and sale of European captives.
Its French nickname, Bastion des Larmes ('Bastion of Tears'), is tied to Salé's grim memory as a corsair port where captives were held and traded.
It sits on the right (Salé) bank, opposite Rabat's Kasbah of the Oudayas across the estuary.
legend: The name 'Bastion of Tears' is popularly linked to the weeping of captives shipped through the corsair port – a folk gloss rather than a documented event.
w: The 'Bastion of Tears' on the Sale bank, a 17th-century fort over the river mouth -- raised in the age of the Sallee Rovers and the corsair Republic of Bou Regreg, which ran on piracy, ransom and captives. Its grim nickname is folk gloss, tied to the weeping of those shipped through the port. A pirate stronghold, facing the Oudayas.

hook: The only kasbah on the Salé side of the river, raised by Moulay Ismail and garrisoned by his Black Guard to watch the corsair coast.
facts: Also called the Kasbah of the Gnaouas or the Ismailian Kasbah, founded in 1708 under the Alaouite sultan Moulay Ismail.
It is the only kasbah on the right (Salé) bank of the Bou Regreg.
It was garrisoned by the Jaysh Abid al-Bukhari – Moulay Ismail's Black Guard drawn from sub-Saharan slave-soldiers – to defend the coasts of Salé and Rabat against foreign incursion.
Its name links it to the Gnaoua, descendants of sub-Saharan peoples whose music and brotherhood remain part of Moroccan culture.
w: The only kasbah on the Sale bank, founded by Sultan Moulay Ismail in 1708 and garrisoned by his Black Guard -- the Abid al-Bukhari, slave-soldiers drawn from sub-Saharan Africa, set to watch the corsair coast. Its name ties it to the Gnaoua, whose descendants' music still threads through Morocco. A fort named for the men who held it.
